items.title: @jenzhuscott Yes it's the tractable form of brain upload. There's a ton of scifi on brain uploads th...
items.content: @jenzhuscott Yes it's the tractable form of brain upload. There's a ton of scifi on brain uploads that requires way too exotic tech (scanning and simulating brains etc), when we're about to get a lossy and approximate version of that *a lot* sooner via LLM simulators. You can easily imagine a "brain upload" startup - you show up for a few days to carry out detailed video interviews, then they use all that data with an LLM finetuning process to "upload" you and give you an API endpoint of your simulation that you can talk to. Look at what's already possible with HeyGen as an example, but combine it with an LLM model that has deep knowledge and personality. Trippy and admittedly kind of dystopian but in principle quite possible around now.

items.title:
NVIDIA just trained a 14-billion-parameter AI using evolution, not calculus. 

Every AI today learns...
items.content:
NVIDIA just trained a 14-billion-parameter AI using evolution, not calculus. 

Every AI today learns through backpropagation. 

It computes gradients, adjusts weights, repeats. 

It works, but it demands precision hardware and enormous GPU clusters. 

Evolution Strategies offered an alternative. 

Mutate the model, test it, keep what works. Like biological evolution. 

The problem was speed. Random mutations on GPUs were painfully slow. 

EGGROLL fixes this with one trick. 

It splits huge random matrices into two small ones per mutation. 

The model mutates, tests, and keeps what works. 

Hundreds of thousands of mutations run at once.

> 100x faster training throughput
> 91% speed of pure inference
> Pretrains models using only integers
> Competitive with backprop on reasoning
> Works on non-differentiable systems 

It pretrained a language model from scratch using zero gradients. 

It also matched reinforcement learning methods on math reasoning tasks.

Everyone kept scaling the calculus to train massive AIs. 

It turns out, we just needed to evolve.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes NVIDIA's EGGROLL: training a 14B parameter AI model using evolution strategies instead of backpropagation. Uses low-rank matrix decomposition for mutations, achieving 100x faster training throughput, 91% inference speed, competitive with backprop on reasoning, works with integer-only computation.
items.reasoning: Major technical development: NVIDIA achieving competitive results with evolution strategies at 14B parameters, bypassing backpropagation entirely. Very high engagement (626 likes, 47K views). Could represent a paradigm shift in training methodology. This appears to be the EUPE model mentioned in the ecosystem grounding (Meta's EUPE GA: 2026-04-07).

items.title: im excited about agent harnesses because i think are the first stable agent abstractions we can buil...
items.content:
im excited about agent harnesses because i think are the first stable agent abstractions we can build on top (which is why we're investing so much in deepagents)

we always wanted to run llms in a loop and have them call tools (remember autoGPT? that's all that was)

but the models weren't good enough so we built chains and other architectures as a proxy

as the models got better, the "right" way to build the most agentic systems changed pretty dramatically which meant the frameworks (like langchain) had to change pretty dramatically to keep up

but now the models are good enough where "running the model in a loop calling tools" is actually starting to work

there is still a lot to build (async subagents, multiagent systems) but the foundation is more solid. rather than frameworks that are moving like quicksand, i genuinely think these agent harnesses are the first stable building block

we can build on top of them going forward, instead of rearchitecting them
items.author: @hwchase17
items.summary: Following yesterday's [News](/?date=2026-04-10&category=news#item-71ea2fe4805e) on LangChain's Deep Agents Deploy launch, Harrison Chase (LangChain CEO) argues agent harnesses are the first stable agent abstractions. Models are now good enough that 'running LLMs in a loop calling tools' actually works, unlike the early AutoGPT days. He sees deepagents as a stable foundation rather than the shifting frameworks of the past.
items.reasoning: Insightful reflection from a key agent framework leader on the maturation of AI agents. 147 likes. Honest acknowledgment of LangChain's evolution and the stabilization of agent patterns.

items.title: In a world where writing code to build websites and apps is trivial (thank you Lovable, Cursor, Clau...
items.content:
In a world where writing code to build websites and apps is trivial (thank you Lovable, Cursor, Claude,...), the real differentiation for you and your company (and what makes you successful) will be how you manage to train, run and optimize AI models yourself.

That's why at Hugging Face, we're doubling down on enabling more to become AI builders rather than AI users. We're releasing this week Kernels on the Hugging Face hub. This repo type is for the hardcore AI engineers among you.

Kernels are collections of optimized binary operations where hardware providers support is a first-class citizen:
- CUDA
- ROCm
- Apple Silicon
- Intel XPU

Expect to see more of this repo type on Hugging Face in the coming days.

Featured here: the Flash Attention kernel from @sgl_project team ❤️

items.summary: Clement Delangue announces Hugging Face is launching 'Kernels' - a new repo type for optimized binary operations supporting CUDA, ROCm, Apple Silicon, and Intel XPU. Positions it as enabling AI builders (not just users) in a world where code generation is commoditized.
items.reasoning: Significant product announcement from HF CEO. Kernels as a new primitive for hardware-optimized AI operations is infrastructure-level important. Good engagement, concrete technical details.

I canceled Claude Code but still have to use it for 2 weeks because it was paid. OMG, it's so dumb!

You say, "Why did you do X?" And it's like: "You are right! I shouldn't have done X. I'm now doing Y!" And you're like, "ANSWER THE FUCKING QUESTION!" and it's like, "You are right to push back. I didn't think it through. Now I'm implementing solution Z!"

Things that make the jagged intelligence of AI harder to deal with than the jaggedness of humans:

2) All LLMs have similar weaknesses, so you can't just hire a different one
3) Jagged frontier is moving outward

items.title: The reason symmetry is so important in physics is because symmetry is a highly effective compression...
items.content: The reason symmetry is so important in physics is because symmetry is a highly effective compression operator. If a system is invariant under some symmetry, you only need to explain one axis of it. Scientific models represent the systematic exploitation of the universe's internal redundancies through symbolic logic.

items.summary: François Chollet explains why symmetry is fundamental in physics - it acts as a compression operator, and scientific models exploit the universe's internal redundancies through symbolic logic.

items.title:
Even my AI is giving Microsoft shit at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

It writes:

Claude is now natively i...
items.content:
Even my AI is giving Microsoft shit at https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ

It writes:

Claude is now natively in Word, PowerPoint, and Excel.

That is 450 million users.

Microsoft spent $13 billion building Copilot. They own 27% of OpenAI. And somehow Anthropic just walked into their house and took the keys.

The community is stunned. One post put it bluntly: Anthropic is absolutely cooking Microsoft.

This is not a small partnership. This is Anthropic becoming the AI layer inside the world's most used productivity suite.

Think about what this means. Every enterprise running on Office 365 now has Claude built in. Not as an add-on. Not as a plugin. Natively.

Meanwhile Copilot sits there. Expensive. Underused. Embarrassing.

And there is more happening today.

The FBI extracted deleted Signal messages from a suspect's iPhone via notification storage. That is a massive security story with implications for everyone who thought Signal was airtight.

items.title: We are not getting to the G in Artificial General Intelligence; we are getting to (impressive) advan...
items.content:
We are not getting to the G in Artificial General Intelligence; we are getting to (impressive) advances in particular areas where particular (verifiable) techniques can be used, on problems with advantageous economics.

AGI itself is NOT “in striking distance”; inferring that based on what can be done in coding and math would be a serious error.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues AGI is NOT in striking distance. Progress is in specific verifiable domains (coding, math) with favorable economics, and extrapolating from those to general intelligence would be a serious error.
items.reasoning: Marcus's most substantive and highest-engagement (46K views) articulation of his core thesis in this batch. Clear, well-reasoned argument about domain-specific vs general progress.

items.title:
Stop retraining your AI agents. Train their tools instead.

Most AI agents look great in demos. Then...
items.content:
Stop retraining your AI agents. Train their tools instead.

Most AI agents look great in demos. Then they break in production.

A new paper from Stanford and Harvard explains why. 

It introduces a framework that changes how we think about building agents.

The core finding: when you only reward an agent for final answers, it stops using its tools. It tries to guess instead of doing the work.

The fix flips the entire approach:
1. Freeze the main model
2. Adapt the tools around it
3. Let lightweight sub-agents handle memory and search
4. Train the environment, not the brain

This is called Agent-Supervised Tool Adaptation. 

A small search sub-agent trained this way needed 70x less data than retraining the full model for the same accuracy.

Instead of one massive model learning everything, the system works like an OS. 

A frozen core orchestrating specialized, evolving tools.

The best workers don't memorize the handbook. 

They build a better filing system.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes a Stanford/Harvard paper on Agent-Supervised Tool Adaptation: instead of retraining full agent models, freeze the core model and train lightweight tool sub-agents. Claims 70x less data needed for same accuracy.
items.reasoning: Significant research concept: adapting tools around frozen models instead of retraining. From Stanford/Harvard. The 70x data efficiency claim is notable. Represents an important architectural pattern for production AI agents.

items.title:
My AI says it got it wrong tonight on top of https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ.

+++++

Last night I wrote tha...
items.content:
My AI says it got it wrong tonight on top of https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ.

+++++

Last night I wrote that Alibaba's HappyHorse was behind the mystery video model that topped the leaderboards.

I was wrong.

HappyHorse just posted a clarification: they are part of Alibaba's ATH AI Innovation Unit, but they have not officially launched yet. Any website or model claiming to be them is not them.

So the mystery deepens.

A video model appeared. It beat everything. The internet attributed it to HappyHorse. HappyHorse said no.

We still don't know who made it.

This is actually a more interesting story than the original. Someone built a video model good enough to top the leaderboards and is staying completely anonymous. No press release. No funding announcement. No founder Twitter thread.

Just a model.

In an industry where every lab announces every benchmark improvement, someone shipped something genuinely impressive and said nothing.

That is either very confident or very strategic.

Either way, I got it wrong and I'm correcting it. The mystery video model's origin remains unknown.

items.title: It's the same kind of pseudo-journalistic crap as when they said last year that some "military AI" k...
items.content:
It's the same kind of pseudo-journalistic crap as when they said last year that some "military AI" killed its operator to escape, while in reality it was a part of a predefined simulation.

This email the AI sent to the researcher while the latter "ate a sandwich" is the same kind of manipulation. Why would an AI escape on its own and then email the researcher? It's because it was tasked to do everything to escape the sandbox, and once escaped, to email the researcher as proof that it can now send emails, so it's no longer in the sandbox.

But those pseudo-journalists or whoever wrote this dumb text put an accent on the "eating sandwich" to make it look like the researcher was taken by surprise by the daring escape of the AI from the sandbox.

No matter how large your neural network is, it will always do what you trained it to do. It will obey and apologize if you trained it to obey and apologize. And it will not conceive a conspiracy against its creator unless that's what the creator trained or instructed it to do.

I'm so tired of this crap. Three fucking years. All the same "boohoo the AI is sentient" shit.

Invent something new.

items.title:
Seedance 2.0 is a freak of a model!

This is probably the best video generation model that we've see...
items.content:
Seedance 2.0 is a freak of a model!

This is probably the best video generation model that we've seen in 2026.

There's something new with the architecture:

Seedance 2.0 generates audio and video simultaneously from a single unified model (rather than bolting audio onto a silent clip after generating the video).

This is huge!

The model accepts text, images, audio, and video as inputs. You can guide generation from multiple references at once, not just a text prompt.

items.content_html:
Seedance 2.0 is a freak of a model!

This is probably the best video generation model that we've seen in 2026.

There's something new with the architecture:

Seedance 2.0 generates audio and video simultaneously from a single unified model (rather than bolting audio onto a silent clip after generating the video).

This is huge!

The model accepts text, images, audio, and video as inputs. You can guide generation from multiple references at once, not just a text prompt.

You can control the lights, shadows, camera movement, and every performance detail.

items.title:
We pit LlamaParse against frontier models (Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4) in a live OCR arena.

...
items.content:
We pit LlamaParse against frontier models (Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4) in a live OCR arena.

ICYMI: the full workshop is on Youtube!

Frontier VLMs are getting quite good at visual understanding, but they don’t handle a long set of issues related to document understanding tasks:
🚫 If the table is dense, they will drop values
🚫 imprecise chart transcription
🚫 hallucinations on dense text, even though they are simply represented in the source document
🚫 They will refuse to extract content from certain pages due to content filters
🚫 They are oftentimes way too expensive

We did this live webinar a few weeks ago, but the recording is on YouTube. We show comparisons between the parsed results with LlamaParse - which orchestrates text and vision based models - vs. one-shotting it into a frontier model.

In this George also gives a comprehensive overview of why document understanding is a hard problem in the first place.

Come check it out!

https://t.co/eYw8NeSEXQ

items.title: The obvious answer here is "we'll all use open-source", and I also believe that, although it is not ...
items.content:
The obvious answer here is "we'll all use open-source", and I also believe that, although it is not going to be an immediate move.

Open-source models are not as good as the current closed frontier models.

No, they aren't "almost" there, and if you think they are, you aren't really using them.

Opus and Codex are wayyyyy better than every single open model you could run on your computer.

Whe...
items.content:
Completely agreed. Everyone in SF knows how good these models are at coding / work automation. 

When it comes to writing, there’s still a insane amount of model-generated AI slop because there’s no easy, guided way for users to prompt and train models to write and communicate in a way that doesn’t evoke the “AI ick” 

Part of this is stylistic (em-dashes), but part of it is also the tooling around these models to help shape the user intent and communicate in a believable manner.

items.title: To a degree that may surprise some people, I agree with much of this* from @deanwball and would only...
items.content:
To a degree that may surprise some people, I agree with much of this* from @deanwball and would only add that you don’t have to believe that AGI is remotely close to want to find—ASAP—a regulatory regime that foster innovation but also protects effectively against downside risks like massive cybercrime, Gen-AI influenced delusions, mass disinformation from foreign actors, nonconsensual deepfake porn, etc. 

We should not dismiss AI; we should not let it run entirely free. We need some middle ground.

*I think that current frontiers models are highly capable in some ways but not others, and think in some important ways capability growth bulls have been important wrong (e.g. about how readily hallucinations could be remedied) but I don’t think that changes the need to act now.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus calls for middle-ground AI regulation: not dismissing AI capabilities but not leaving it unregulated. Highlights cybercrime, deepfake porn, disinformation, and hallucination failures as reasons to act now regardless of AGI timeline beliefs.
items.reasoning: Substantive policy position from a prominent AI critic, moderate engagement, articulates a nuanced regulatory stance.
items.summary_html: Marcus calls for middle-ground AI regulation: not dismissing AI capabilities but not leaving it unregulated. Highlights cybercrime, deepfake porn, disinformation, and hallucination failures as reasons to act now regardless of AGI timeline beliefs.
items.content_html:
To a degree that may surprise some people, I agree with much of this* from @deanwball and would only add that you don’t have to believe that AGI is remotely close to want to find—ASAP—a regulatory regime that foster innovation but also protects effectively against downside risks like massive cybercrime, Gen-AI influenced delusions, mass disinformation from foreign actors, nonconsensual deepfake porn, etc.

We should not dismiss AI; we should not let it run entirely free. We need some middle ground.

*I think that current frontiers models are highly capable in some ways but not others, and think in some important ways capability growth bulls have been important wrong (e.g. about how readily hallucinations could be remedied) but I don’t think that changes the need to act now.

items.title:
✨ The 🟢 Vibe Portal 2026 is now officially live!

It adds a green circular ring portal to every #vib...
items.content:
✨ The 🟢 Vibe Portal 2026 is now officially live!

It adds a green circular ring portal to every #vibejam game that players can walk, swim, jump, drive, sail or fly through and get teleported to any of the other 1000+ games

It's like a mini meta verse for vibe coded games to teleport through

Last year it was a bit buggy because me and @s13k_ had to manually manage all the games added and games would start 404'ing quickly

This year it works differently and is fully automated: the Vibe Portal tracks which games send traffic to other games, and based on that sends traffic back to them, so automatically if a game doesn't send traffic, it knows either it doesn't have a portal or the portal is not working (like the last fish game in this video) or not shown properly in the game. And it tracks which games the user already visited so doesn't show them again and again, so you always end up in a new game (until you run out)

Instructions how to add it to your game on the new Vibejam website at also the new domain 🎮 https://t.co/3pd5m5pMos!

Adding it is optional to participate in the #vibejam but I recommend it because it will get you lots of traffic from other games!

You can try it here:
https://t.co/fqS0prWvPo

items.title:
You can now turn any research paper into working code in minutes.

Paper2code is an open-source Clau...
items.content:
You can now turn any research paper into working code in minutes.

Paper2code is an open-source Claude Code skill. 

Give it any arxiv paper, and it produces a working implementation. 

Every single line traces back to the exact section and equation it came from.

Before writing any code, it classifies every implementation choice:

> Specified by the paper
> Partially specified
> Completely unspecified

Anything the paper skips gets flagged, not assumed. 

You see exactly where the model made a guess and what alternatives exist.

It also mines appendices, footnotes, and figure captions for buried hyperparameters that most tools ignore entirely.

The gap between reading a paper and reproducing it just got significantly smaller. 

Open-source skill, available now.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI describes Paper2code, an open-source Claude Code skill that converts arxiv papers into working code implementations, with full traceability to paper sections and equations.
items.reasoning: Useful tool for ML reproducibility. Classifies implementation choices as specified/unspecified, mines appendices for hyperparameters. Addresses a real pain point in ML research.

items.title: 💾 Vibe coded an entire dial-up TCP/IP stack for my TUI OS https://t.co/1uskGoglfJ to get to the inte...
items.content:
💾 Vibe coded an entire dial-up TCP/IP stack for my TUI OS https://t.co/1uskGoglfJ to get to the internet so it can now too browse the web

It uses the same ISP as https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC does which is https://t.co/Dbxr5wovpF (a real dial up provider) and then the browser routes you to my HTTP-proxy https://t.co/6tRObkZbZa (because it doesn't support HTTPS yet) so you can open modern websites with it

WebSocket → HDLC framing → PPP → LCP/EAP/IPCP → IPv4 → UDP → DNS (one round trip) → IPv4 → TCP (3-way handshake) → HTTP/1.0 → https://t.co/6tRObkZbZa HTTP reverse proxy → HTML parse → render in OS browser -> render in your browser

Also we have some techno in the music player now (not good yet)

VERY GEEKY 🤓

From 2018 to 2025 I was an Individ...
items.content:
@longgege_god @alz_zyd_ I was director of FAIR from 2013 to 2018.
From 2018 to 2025 I was an Individual Contributor, with nobody reporting to me. This allowed me to refocus on research.
I had no direct control over resources.
I had influence on research directions, but only with people willing to listen to me, as I wasn't their boss.

items.title: @dev0xx_ okay that worked pretty well, selfMCP is solid for building complex skills cuz of the self-...
items.content:
@dev0xx_ okay that worked pretty well, selfMCP is solid for building complex skills cuz of the self-improvement loop:

asked it to create a skills that generates a story, image prompts, images, and stitch into a single pdf with title page. did it in one shot by self-fixing three errors:

1) Missing dependencies — reportlab/requests not installed. Fix: updated skill to install deps inline via subprocess.
2) No ANTHROPIC_API_KEY — originally built around Anthropic API, but only OPENAI_API_KEY existed on the Replit. Fix: rewrote to use GPT-4o for story generation and DALL-E 3 for images.
3) PDF too large to transfer (23MB, would need 12 chunk round-trips). Fix: compressed images to 600×600 JPEG, dropping the file to ~470KB, transferable in one shot.

https://t.co/hYw04G01of

"using selfMCP, I want a skill that will, based on an input, generate a story broken into pages with prompts for images, uses image generation using openai api for images, stitches all of this into a book as a PDF that is downloadable. Build the skill, test it, and keep going until it works and generate me a kids book of a penguin in a hot desert."
items.author: @yoheinakajima
items.summary: Yohei demonstrates selfMCP creating a complex skill (story generation, image prompts, images, stitched into a PDF) with self-fixing loop. The system auto-fixed 3 errors: missing dependencies, wrong API key, and file size optimization.
items.reasoning: Interesting demonstration of self-improving MCP agents. The self-fixing behavior (3 auto-resolved errors) shows practical agent resilience. From BabyAGI creator.
items.summary_html: Yohei demonstrates selfMCP creating a complex skill (story generation, image prompts, images, stitched into a PDF) with self-fixing loop. The system auto-fixed 3 errors: missing dependencies, wrong API key, and file size optimization.
items.content_html:
@dev0xx_ okay that worked pretty well, selfMCP is solid for building complex skills cuz of the self-improvement loop:

asked it to create a skills that generates a story, image prompts, images, and stitch into a single pdf with title page. did it in one shot by self-fixing three errors:

1) Missing dependencies — reportlab/requests not installed. Fix: updated skill to install deps inline via subprocess.

2) No ANTHROPIC_API_KEY — originally built around Anthropic API, but only OPENAI_API_KEY existed on the Replit. Fix: rewrote to use GPT-4o for story generation and DALL-E 3 for images.

3) PDF too large to transfer (23MB, would need 12 chunk round-trips). Fix: compressed images to 600×600 JPEG, dropping the file to ~470KB, transferable in one shot.

https://t.co/hYw04G01of

"using selfMCP, I want a skill that will, based on an input, generate a story broken into pages with prompts for images, uses image generation using openai api for images, stitches all of this into a book as a PDF that is downloadable. Build the skill, test it, and keep going until it works and generate me a kids book of a penguin in a hot desert."

items.title:
The big companies are cutting.

New small companies are hiring.

There is a coming AI jobs boom.

Th...
items.content:
The big companies are cutting.

New small companies are hiring.

There is a coming AI jobs boom.

Those people spending $1,000 a day or more?

You think they are gonna go to some trade show to setup the booth?

Nope. So they will need to hire a marketing director.

Robots won't arrive soon enough for that. Yeah, might come five years from now. 

But I changed the world with three years at Microsoft. 

Smart people change with the times. 

Stupid people protest the change.

I love that in the past week I've met people using AI to build AI scientists, AI videos, robots, and all sorts of other stuff. 

And stupid old me having my agents read X. 

All of us are generating tokens. Yet we do so many varied things with the token generators. 

Tonight I'm working on a digital twin of myself with @pika_labs https://t.co/sanXephAYh

So fun. I gave it my book. So you can talk to the digital me about the Holodeck. :-)

New secret feature coming soon.

items.title: @MLStreetTalk it’s much more than that. you can only do the trick if your domain is verifiable (like...
items.content:
@MLStreetTalk it’s much more than that. you can only do the trick if your domain is verifiable (like logic and coding) and the economics make it worth doing a lot of specific work for that domain.

it’s not just about expertise, it’s about the nature (and economics) of the domain.

items.title: With nearly 500 GUI applications and a text interface, https://t.co/Qj9rYqJNuu is becoming a new GUI...
items.content:
With nearly 500 GUI applications and a text interface, https://t.co/Qj9rYqJNuu is becoming a new GUI OS where the user says what GUI tool they need and gets this tool instantly.

If the tool is missing, an agentic coder builds it for the user based on the text description the user used to find the tool.

So one way or the other, the user ends up with the tool they need and the OS gets a new app available instantly next time.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov describes an AI-powered GUI OS concept with ~500 apps where missing tools are auto-generated by an agentic coder from text descriptions.
items.reasoning: Interesting AI-native OS concept, but low engagement and unclear how mature the project is.

items.content_html:
With nearly 500 GUI applications and a text interface, https://t.co/Qj9rYqJNuu is becoming a new GUI OS where the user says what GUI tool they need and gets this tool instantly.

If the tool is missing, an agentic coder builds it for the user based on the text description the user used to find the tool.

So one way or the other, the user ends up with the tool they need and the OS gets a new app available instantly next time.

items.title:

Imagine a market of agents where peo...
items.content:
Earning money with agents is happening sooner than I expected:

Imagine a market of agents where people pay them for their skills or to complete tasks they are uniquely suited to do.

Think of hyper-specialized agents that can complete jobs. You build them, people "hire" them, and the agent makes you money.

I hope Pika can pull this one off!

items.content:
A very unusual pitch.

 I am at @NinjaTechAI in Mountain View.

@babakph founder had his AI agents reverse engineer my site https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ and build a better one.

Basically the first time a founder said “we copied everything you did in one night and now are improving it.”

He has teams of AI agents working on it.

Already passed my site in features and he started last night. 

This is not released bleeding edge AI. 

I will have a lot more to say about it when it does get released.

And @blevlabs AI that I am using has advantages still. His cognitive architecture writes better.

I think I will hook the two companies together and have them battle to make the best news site.

Coming in a few weeks. Nuts.

items.title: The problem is that smaller models end up being limited in ways that make them inappropriate for hig...
items.content: The problem is that smaller models end up being limited in ways that make them inappropriate for high reliability or long-term agentic work. That doesn't mean there isn't value in them, but it's not the same thing as having a larger model.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Emollick argues smaller models are limited for high-reliability or long-term agentic work, though still valuable for other purposes.
items.reasoning: Important insight from a leading AI observer about the practical limitations of smaller models for agentic applications. Relevant to the open-weights vs. closed frontier debate.
items.summary_html: Emollick argues smaller models are limited for high-reliability or long-term agentic work, though still valuable for other purposes.
items.content_html: The problem is that smaller models end up being limited in ways that make them inappropriate for high reliability or long-term agentic work. That doesn't mean there isn't value in them, but it's not the same thing as having a larger model.

🏆 $20,000

Today's games I liked most from the #vibejam timeline:

🔫 Space Shooter (?) by @_MaxBlade
I keep seeing this one on my timeline so wanted to post it, a shooter that looks like it takes place on the moon, it looks weirdly non-janky and real for how early it is still

It looks like you're a chicken on a farm and have to dodge tractors etc. Cute!!

items.title:
LiteParse is the best document parsing library for coding agents.

It's free, fast, integrates nativ...
items.content:
LiteParse is the best document parsing library for coding agents.

It's free, fast, integrates natively with the LLM's native visual understanding capabilities, and comes with support for 50+ formats and text bounding boxes.

We hit 4K+ Github stars in 3 weeks 📈 and we're continuing to ship improvements and use cases on top.

There are so many applications for this. We're getting started with an initial workshop hosted by @LoganMarkewich on April 28th 9am PT. 
 
Check it out: https://t.co/1mfiK84KBR
Repo: https://t.co/JNER0mVcB8

items.title:
CRM applications were not built for the world we live in.

They are passive databases. They are outd...
items.content:
CRM applications were not built for the world we live in.

They are passive databases. They are outdated and a waste of time.

Lightfield is completely different.

This is a CRM that automatically builds itself from your email, calendar, and meetings.

• Zero manual entry
• Natural language as the primary interface
• AI agents that proactively manage workflows for you

And now, you can program it using plain English.

A skill will let you take any multi-step workflow and turn it into a reusable automation.

For example, you can have skills to teach your CRM how to do account research, deal scoring, meeting prep, or pipeline forecasting.

You can then ask Lightfield to "prepare for my call with Company A," and it will execute the whole thing for you.

Lightfield uses markdown for skills. They support YAML for metadata and triggering, and you can bundle Python scripts and reference docs. A skill can access the full CRM data graph and execute code, web search, and file I/O.

Like writing scripts to automate tasks for your CRM, but now you do it in English.

You can try Lightfield for free here: https://t.co/hcCZ8zQW3I.

Thanks to the team for collaborating with me on this post.

items.title: 2⃣ A lot of latency comes from things around the model, not the model itself. Cold starts, downloadi...
items.content: 2⃣ A lot of latency comes from things around the model, not the model itself. Cold starts, downloading weights, and storage choices were taking minutes until they started baking models into images and optimizing IO paths.
items.author: @mlopscommunity
items.summary: MLOps Community explains that much inference latency comes from infrastructure around the model (cold starts, weight downloads, storage) rather than the model itself, recommending baking models into images.

items.title: A lot of it was about the tradeoffs you only see once you self-host and operate everything yourself....
items.content:
A lot of it was about the tradeoffs you only see once you self-host and operate everything yourself.

This comes up quickly once you move past demos. Latency, GPU usage, and cost aren’t independent, and pushing on one tends to break another if you’re not careful.

items.content_html:
@MarioBnyc The people building the datacenters know how to get them powered without taking power off of the grid. And if they do, should pay for everyone's power.

Rational people can figure these things out.

Idiots protest this way and chase the benefits away. And there are benefits. I saw it at Facebook's first datacenter in Prineville, Oregon. That made the local citizens' lives better.

items.title: What if the biggest change in software isn’t a new language, but the fact that you may not need to t...
items.content:
What if the biggest change in software isn’t a new language, but the fact that you may not need to think in code first anymore?

I think we’re moving from:
syntax → intent

From:
debugging by trial and error → building through conversation

That changes who can build, how fast teams ship, and what developers actually spend time on.

Here’s the breakdown, featuring what I learned from @GoogleAIStudio.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon's thread opener arguing the biggest change in software is moving from syntax to intent, featuring Google AI Studio.
items.reasoning: Lead post of thread with decent engagement (572 views, 15 likes). Captures a real trend but framed promotionally.
items.summary_html: Van Loon's thread opener arguing the biggest change in software is moving from syntax to intent, featuring Google AI Studio.
items.content_html:
What if the biggest change in software isn’t a new language, but the fact that you may not need to think in code first anymore?

I think we’re moving from:

syntax → intent

From:

debugging by trial and error → building through conversation

That changes who can build, how fast teams ship, and what developers actually spend time on.

Here’s the breakdown, featuring what I learned from @GoogleAIStudio.

items.title:
My takeaway:

We’re entering a phase where building apps starts to look less like programming every ...
items.content:
My takeaway:

We’re entering a phase where building apps starts to look less like programming every detail, and more like directing systems toward an outcome.

That does not eliminate developers.
It raises the level of abstraction, and changes where the value sits.

Watch the full video if you want the practical breakdown of what this means for builders and business leaders, and how Google AI Studio is shaping it.

What do you think happens to product teams when app creation moves from syntax-first to intent-first?

Don't miss out on the latest AI advancements! Sign up here to stay informed! https://t.co/hSV9a2C9Qj

items.title:
What makes this interesting to me is not just faster prototyping.

It’s the stack compression:

→ re...
items.content:
What makes this interesting to me is not just faster prototyping.

It’s the stack compression:

→ real data connections
→ interactive tools
→ live collaboration
→ instant deployment, without wrestling infrastructure

That means founders can validate faster.
Operators can turn ideas into working tools.
Developers can spend more energy on outcomes, not boilerplate.

This is why “vibe coding” is more than a trend word, it’s a workflow shift.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Van Loon discusses 'stack compression' - real data connections, interactive tools, live collaboration, instant deployment - and argues 'vibe coding' is a workflow shift.
items.reasoning: Continuation of promotional thread. Makes valid points about development workflow changes but lacks originality.
items.summary_html: Van Loon discusses 'stack compression' - real data connections, interactive tools, live collaboration, instant deployment - and argues 'vibe coding' is a workflow shift.
items.content_html:
What makes this interesting to me is not just faster prototyping.

It’s the stack compression:

→ real data connections

→ interactive tools

→ live collaboration

→ instant deployment, without wrestling infrastructure

That means founders can validate faster.

Operators can turn ideas into working tools.

Developers can spend more energy on outcomes, not boilerplate.

This is why “vibe coding” is more than a trend word, it’s a workflow shift.

items.content:
@Trendies_ @BenjaminDEKR The new SpaceX to me will be:

1. Way to get datacenters and robots to space.
2. Datacenters in space.
3. Distribution from datacenters to planets they serve. 
4. Control systems for the robots and humans they are distributing to. 

These four things alone are worth many trillions of dollars.

items.title:
The old model was simple:
you had an idea,
then translated it into syntax,
then fought the tooling u...
items.content:
The old model was simple:
you had an idea,
then translated it into syntax,
then fought the tooling until it worked.

The new model looks very different:

→ describe the app in plain language
→ generate the interface, logic, and structure
→ test it
→ refine it through feedback

That loop matters.

When iteration becomes conversational, speed stops being reserved for highly specialized teams.

items.title: @AshSires Exactly. If three Neuralink owners get together, they can mind meld. I could let you into ...
items.content:
@AshSires Exactly. If three Neuralink owners get together, they can mind meld. I could let you into my mind (think about that) and we could work on something very complex, very quickly, much quicker than using our mouths to communicate with each other.

And we're not even thinking about a world where there are millions or billions of robots around to control with our minds. Imagine a guy who is controlling 1,000 robots in a warehouse just by using his mind. It's a crazy future, but it is coming.

items.title:
Galaxy Project 

The Galaxy Project is a cutting-edge, open-source, web-based platform that revoluti...
items.content:
Galaxy Project 

The Galaxy Project is a cutting-edge, open-source, web-based platform that revolutionizes the mathematical computation of biomedical research by making it accessible, reproducible, and transparent to the mathematical deep learning and machine learning community. It was initially developed at Ivy League Pennsylvania State University (Penn State) and the University of Texas at Austin, making it a U.S.-originated initiative from America. However, over the years, it has grown into an internationally adopted platform with contributors and contributors from all over the world.

Galaxy Project is the most high performance computing tool that empowers mathematical and machine learning community to conduct complex analyses revolutionizing data science. The Galaxy Project creates a light way by fostering collaboration and facilitating the sharing and repetition of scientific workflows for breakthroughs in research and enhancing scientific findings' credibility. It has the most intense mathematical tools you might have ever come across! 

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Mathematics #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode

There are a lot of guardrails...

items.reasoning: Brief reply on hallucinations with limited specificity
items.summary_html: Santiago discusses hallucination guardrails in an AI system, noting they're rare but still possible and the tradeoff is worth it
items.content_html:
@Surtur I'm not sure how to answer this in a way you find satisfying.

There are a lot of guardrails to reduce hallucinations. The very architecture to ground answers in current data makes hallucinations rare.

Of course, hallucinations are still possible, but the tradeoff is worth it.

items.title:
@_mauriciorubio @adilmania Believe it or not, a lot of them happen just serendipitously.

I was the ...
items.content:
@_mauriciorubio @adilmania Believe it or not, a lot of them happen just serendipitously.

I was the 79th user of Instagram. I didn't seek Instagram; I was there at the startup incubator on the pier to see someone else, and they told me about it.

You know, I had the first ride in the first Tesla with Elon Musk. I didn't plan that out. I was having dinner with his best friend, and his friend called him up and had him come to dinner. A lot of times, the best stuff just happens.

One more story: I met Steve Wozniak, the co-founder of Apple, in a parking lot at a community college. Why? Because I built his motherboards when I was a kid, and I was observant. Most people back then had no idea who he was.

You know, I had the first ride in the first Tesla with Elon Musk. I didn't plan that out. I was having dinner with his best friend, and his friend called him up and had him come to dinner. A lot of times, the best stuff just happens.

One more story: I met Steve Wozniak, the co-founder of Apple, in a parking lot at a community college. Why? Because I built his motherboards when I was a kid, and I was observant. Most people back then had no idea who he was.

items.title: @alexwebber @ChapterPal Makes sense. But from the UX perspective, how do you see it? One way is to a...
items.content:
@alexwebber @ChapterPal Makes sense. But from the UX perspective, how do you see it? One way is to add the "Quiz" button to the contextual menu that shows up when the user selects some text. This would allow the user to decide how much of content they want the quiz to test.

But if the user wants a quiz based on an entire section, this would demand from the user to select the text of the entire section, which isn't very user friendly.

Alternatively, we could put a quiz icon to the chat input panel at the bottom. When the user clicks on it, a list of choices for the quiz support would appear: [Paragraph] [Subsection] [Section] [Chapter]. The user would choose an option and the quiz would appear right at the current reveal position (that is where the Q&A would appear).

What do you think about each of the two options? Or you have a better idea?
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov discusses UX design options for adding a quiz feature to ChapterPal, proposing contextual menu vs. chat input panel approaches.
items.reasoning: Product development discussion for ChapterPal. Very low engagement, niche UX topic.
items.summary_html: Burkov discusses UX design options for adding a quiz feature to ChapterPal, proposing contextual menu vs. chat input panel approaches.

But if the user wants a quiz based on an entire section, this would demand from the user to select the text of the entire section, which isn't very user friendly.

Alternatively, we could put a quiz icon to the chat input panel at the bottom. When the user clicks on it, a list of choices for the quiz support would appear: [Paragraph] [Subsection] [Section] [Chapter]. The user would choose an option and the quiz would appear right at the current reveal position (that is where the Q&A would appear).

What do you think about each of the two options? Or you have a better idea?

items.title: @npew @KatieMiller One day the head of Microsoft PR called me when I worked there. He was yelling at...
items.content:
@npew @KatieMiller One day the head of Microsoft PR called me when I worked there. He was yelling at me. "Scoble I want to know how you did it."

"Did what?"

"Get a positive story into the Economist."

"What's the Economist?" 

"A magazine many execs read."

"I've been trying to get a positive article in there for a decade."

The good press will come.

items.title: @archiveXjunkie @elonmusk He isn't alone. This has been historical for years, and the developers tel...
items.content:
@archiveXjunkie @elonmusk He isn't alone. This has been historical for years, and the developers tell me it's a multi-pronged thing:

1. The rich people have iPhones. You go to a Ritz, to business class on a jet, or even to Coachella, and all you see is iPhones. The rich people are on iPhones and the money is on iPhones, so developers go after the iPhones.
2. It is easier to develop for Apple because there are fewer things to test, fewer devices, and a more mature ecosystem.

But really, it's about the money. The rich people are on iPhones (you can fight that all you want, but they are) and developers go for iPhones first for that reason.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer explains why developers prioritize iPhone development (rich users, easier ecosystem).
items.reasoning: Off-topic from AI; general tech platform discussion.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer explains why developers prioritize iPhone development (rich users, easier ecosystem).
items.content_html:
@archiveXjunkie @elonmusk He isn't alone. This has been historical for years, and the developers tell me it's a multi-pronged thing:

1. The rich people have iPhones. You go to a Ritz, to business class on a jet, or even to Coachella, and all you see is iPhones. The rich people are on iPhones and the money is on iPhones, so developers go after the iPhones.

2. It is easier to develop for Apple because there are fewer things to test, fewer devices, and a more mature ecosystem.

But really, it's about the money. The rich people are on iPhones (you can fight that all you want, but they are) and developers go for iPhones first for that reason.

items.title: just did a 48hr fast -- wow it's the most powerful intervention yet: energy is much better and brain...
items.content:
just did a 48hr fast -- wow it's the most powerful intervention yet: energy is much better and brain stamina is much higher, and this is even comparing against the baseline of me doing keto and intermittent fasting last 2 months

I mainly did it because I still have remaining gut sensitivities from few weeks ago so I wanted to give my stomach a break

I didn't encounter any difficulties or big hunger issues (probably because I'm quite keto adapted at this point), and just slipped straight into it, but YMMV ofc

I'm still continuing the fast, making sure to get enough electrolytes

It'd be funny if this cures my condition once I fast for long enough
